title: Young Professional: Student Loan Debt and Early Wealth Building
constraints: Negative net worth due to student loans
Lifestyle inflation from recent salary increase
key_risks: Income disruption with inadequate emergency fund
High-interest student loans reducing net worth
Opportunity cost of delaying retirement savings

planning_issues.description: Emergency fund covers only 1.4 months of expenses — below the 3-month minimum
$48,000 student loans at blended 5.8%; highest rate loans (7.0%) should be avalanche-targeted first
Only 6% 401k contribution capturing employer match but not maximizing tax-advantaged space
Monthly surplus of $850 is thin and leaves no room for unexpected expenses without credit card use

key_recommendations: Build $5,000 emergency fund before accelerating loan payoff
Apply debt avalanche: attack highest-rate loans first (7.0%)
Maintain 401k at minimum to capture full employer match
Refinance private loans if credit score supports lower rate
Increase income through professional development or side work
follow_up_questions: What is the breakdown of federal vs. private loans by interest rate?
Has income-driven repayment (IDR) been evaluated for federal loans?
What is the employer match vesting schedule?
Is PSLF (Public Service Loan Forgiveness) applicable given any prior employment?
outcome_summary: Built $8,000 emergency fund in 7 months; refinanced private loans from 7.0% to 5.1%; paid off $12,000 in highest-rate debt in 14 months.
key_lesson: Young professionals with student debt must sequence goals correctly: emergency fund stability first, then debt avalanche, then maximizing tax-advantaged accounts. Refinancing high-rate private loans can save thousands.
